heroOverview: Sealpox is a zoonotic parapoxvirus infection that spreads from infected pinnipeds — seals and sea lions — to humans through direct skin contact, typically causing one or more firm, raised skin lesions on the hands or forearms of marine mammal handlers, veterinarians, and aquarium workers. The causative agent, the sealpox virus (SPPV), is a parapoxvirus closely related to the orf virus of sheep and the pseudocowpox virus of cattle; infections in healthy individuals are self-limited, resolving within 4–8 weeks without specific antiviral therapy. Fewer than 100 human cases have been described in the published medical literature, making sealpox a true occupational rarity. The condition is almost exclusively confined to people with direct occupational or rehabilitation contact with marine mammals, and there is no documented person-to-person transmission.

keyStats.prevalence: Fewer than 100 human cases documented in the published literature; exact global incidence unknown due to underreporting
keyStats.demographics: Almost exclusively affects marine mammal handlers, aquarium staff, veterinarians, and wildlife rehabilitators with direct contact with seals or sea lions
keyStats.avgAge: Reported cases range across working adult ages; no pediatric predilection
keyStats.globalCases: Cases reported from North America, United Kingdom, Europe, Australia, and South Africa — wherever humans work with pinnipeds

definition: Sealpox (ICD-10: B08.62) is a zoonotic skin infection caused by the sealpox virus (SPPV), a double-stranded DNA virus of the family Poxviridae, genus Parapoxvirus. Parapoxviruses are oval-shaped viruses approximately 140–300 nm long with a distinctive spiral surface tubule morphology visible on electron microscopy. SPPV infects seals, sea lions, and walruses, in which it produces papular skin lesions, and is transmitted to humans through direct skin-to-skin contact with infected animals, particularly through abrasions or minor wounds on the handler's hands. The virus enters the epidermis and replicates in keratinocytes, producing the characteristic nodular lesion that evolves through papule, vesicle, pustule, and nodule stages over several weeks. Unlike smallpox or monkeypox, parapoxvirus infections in humans are strictly localized to the inoculation site and do not disseminate systemically in immunocompetent individuals. Regional lymphadenopathy and mild constitutional symptoms may accompany the local lesion but systemic spread is not reported in healthy hosts. Sealpox falls within the scope of infectious disease and occupational medicine specialists, and in some cases dermatology, given its distinctive clinical presentation. The infection is listed under the ICD-10 category for parapoxvirus infections alongside orf, pseudocowpox (milker's nodule), and paravaccinia.

typesClassification.description: A solitary or few firm, painless to mildly painful nodular lesion(s) on the hands or forearms, progressing through papule → vesicle → pustule → nodule stages over 4–8 weeks before spontaneous resolution; the most common presentation.

Occasionally 2–5 lesions develop at separate inoculation sites when multiple skin abrasions or punctures are exposed to infected animal material in the same encounter; each lesion evolves independently.
Rare reports describe larger, more persistent, or atypical lesions in immunocompromised individuals; these may require antiviral treatment and specialist management.

primarySymptoms: A firm, dome-shaped papule at the site of skin contact with the infected seal, typically appearing 3–10 days after exposure on the hands or forearms.
Progression of the initial papule to a vesicle (fluid-filled blister) and then a pustule with central umbilication over the first 1–2 weeks.
Development of a firm, reddish-blue to violaceous nodule 1–3 cm in diameter with a smooth or weeping surface, representing the peak of the lesion's development at 2–4 weeks.
Mild to moderate pain or tenderness at the lesion site, though many lesions are surprisingly painless given their appearance.
Surrounding erythema (skin redness) and mild induration around the nodule, sometimes resembling an abscess or granuloma.
Regional lymphadenopathy — tender, swollen lymph nodes in the axilla or epitrochlear region draining the affected hand or forearm.
Low-grade fever (below 38.5°C) and mild malaise in some patients during the vesiculopustular phase, reflecting local immune activation.
A crusted eschar forming over the nodule as it begins to resolve, typically after week 4–6, followed by gradual flattening and scar formation.
Pruritus (itching) at or around the lesion, particularly during the crusting phase as the skin begins to heal.
Satellite lesions at adjacent inoculation sites in cases involving multiple skin breaches during animal handling.
earlyWarningSigns: A small firm red papule on the hand or forearm appearing 3–10 days after handling a seal or sea lion that had visible skin lesions.
Tenderness of axillary lymph nodes on the same side as the hand lesion.
A history of a bite, scratch, or skin abrasion during pinniped handling — the majority of cases have an identifiable inoculation event.
emergencySigns: Rapidly expanding cellulitis with warmth, spreading redness, and systemic fever above 38.5°C, suggesting secondary bacterial superinfection requiring urgent antibiotic treatment.
Signs of septicemia (high fever, rigors, confusion, low blood pressure) in a patient with a known skin wound from animal handling.
Rapidly enlarging or atypical lesions in an immunocompromised patient, which may indicate a less-restricted parapoxvirus infection requiring specialist assessment.
Any eye splash or ocular exposure from infected seal materials, warranting emergency ophthalmological review.

causes.cause: Direct skin contact with infected seal or sea lion skin lesions
causes.description: The primary transmission route; SPPV is shed from papular or crusty skin lesions on pinnipeds and enters human skin through small abrasions, punctures, or breached skin during animal handling or veterinary procedures.

causes.cause: Bites or scratches from infected animals
causes.description: Seal and sea lion bites and scratches disrupt skin integrity and simultaneously inoculate the wound with virus-laden material from the animal's oral cavity or skin, providing a direct route for SPPV to enter the dermis.

causes.cause: Contact with mucous membranes of infected animals
causes.description: Nasal and oral secretions from pinnipeds with active sealpox lesions contain viral particles; handlers who touch an animal's face and then touch their own skin or mucous membranes before hand-washing can transfer virus.

causes.cause: Fomite transmission from contaminated surfaces or equipment
causes.description: Virus-contaminated handling gloves, equipment, tank surfaces, or clothing can theoretically transmit infection if they contact broken skin, though direct animal contact is the dominant route in reported cases.

causes.cause: Occupational exposure without adequate protective equipment
causes.description: Failure to wear cut-resistant gloves when handling animals with visible skin lesions, or handling infected animals in rehabilitation settings where protective gear is unavailable or not consistently used, significantly raises transmission risk.

riskFactors.description: Virtually all documented human sealpox cases have occurred in people with professional or rehabilitation contact with seals, sea lions, or walruses; this occupational exposure is the defining risk factor and there is no significant community-acquired risk.

riskFactors.factor: Skin abrasions or compromised skin barrier
riskFactors.description: Pre-existing cuts, abrasions, or eczematous skin on the hands significantly increase the chance of viral entry during animal contact; intact skin provides a more effective barrier against parapoxvirus inoculation.

Not wearing appropriate gloves when handling pinnipeds with visible skin lesions removes the primary mechanical barrier to transmission; consistent PPE use has been associated with zero sealpox infections in marine mammal facilities that mandate it.

riskFactors.factor: Handling of animals with active lesions
riskFactors.description: Contact with pinnipeds during active sealpox outbreaks in wildlife populations or aquarium collections dramatically increases exposure dose; viral load is highest during the vesiculopustular phase of animal lesions.

HIV infection, organ transplantation, immunosuppressive therapy, and other conditions impairing cellular immunity may allow the virus to cause larger, more persistent, or atypical lesions; there are a small number of case reports of more severe disease in this setting.

Rehabilitating sick or stranded seals and sea lions — animals under physiological stress and more likely to have active infections — carries higher exposure risk than working with healthy captive-bred animals in well-managed facilities.

affectedDemographics: Marine mammal trainers and handlers at aquariums, zoos, and research facilities
Veterinarians and veterinary technicians treating pinnipeds
Wildlife rehabilitators handling stranded seals and sea lions
Marine biologists and field researchers tagging or sampling wild pinnipeds
Occasionally, members of the public who handle stranded animals without protective equipment
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of sealpox is primarily clinical, based on the combination of a characteristic evolving nodular skin lesion and a clear history of direct pinniped contact within the preceding 3–14 days. The lesion's morphology — progression from papule to vesicle to nodule over weeks on the hand or forearm — is highly characteristic of parapoxvirus infection and may be indistinguishable clinically from orf (caused by Orf virus in sheep handlers) or pseudocowpox (milker's nodule), making exposure history essential for specific diagnosis. Laboratory confirmation, while not always required for management, is achieved by electron microscopy of lesion material, which shows the distinctive elongated, spiral-coated parapoxvirus particles, or by PCR of lesion swab material with sealpox-specific or pan-parapoxvirus primers. Virus isolation in cell culture is possible but technically demanding and limited to specialized reference laboratories. Serology (ELISA or neutralization assay) demonstrates seroconversion but is primarily a research tool rather than a routine clinical diagnostic. In clinical practice, the combination of occupational history and classic lesion morphology is sufficient to make the diagnosis and initiate management. The main diagnostic challenge is excluding secondary bacterial superinfection, which is detected by bacterial culture of lesion swabs when cellulitis or purulent discharge is present.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary diagnostic approach — characteristic evolving nodular lesion plus direct pinniped contact history is sufficient for a working diagnosis in most cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician examines the lesion and asks detailed questions about animal contact, timing, and any protective equipment used. No laboratory test is always required for uncomplicated cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the parapoxvirus diagnosis by visualizing the distinctive elongated, spiral-surface virions approximately 140–300 nm in size; differentiates parapoxvirus from other poxviruses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A scraping or swab of the lesion surface is collected and sent to a reference virology laboratory; results take 1–5 days and require a laboratory with electron microscopy capability.

diagnosticTests.test: PCR of lesion swab (pan-parapoxvirus or SPPV-specific)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Most sensitive molecular confirmation; distinguishes sealpox from orf virus, pseudocowpox, and other parapoxviruses by sequence analysis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab of the lesion surface is taken by the clinician and sent to a virology reference laboratory; results typically take 3–7 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects secondary bacterial superinfection (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus spp.) when cellulitis or purulent discharge is present.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab of the lesion discharge is collected and cultured; results are available in 24–48 hours and guide antibiotic selection if infection is confirmed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: In atypical or persistent cases, skin biopsy demonstrates ballooning degeneration of keratinocytes, intracytoplasmic eosinophilic inclusions, and papillary dermal edema consistent with parapoxvirus infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small punch biopsy is taken under local anesthesia; histopathology results take 3–5 days. The procedure is performed by a dermatologist or surgeon.

treatmentOverview: Sealpox in immunocompetent individuals is a self-limited infection that requires no specific antiviral therapy. The mainstay of management is wound care, protection of the lesion from trauma and secondary infection, and monitoring for complications. The lesion typically resolves spontaneously within 4–8 weeks, passing through papule, vesicle, nodule, and crust stages. Keeping the lesion covered with a clean, non-adherent dressing and avoiding mechanical trauma reduces the risk of secondary bacterial superinfection, the most common complication, which is treated with appropriate oral antibiotics (flucloxacillin, cefalexin, or co-amoxiclav) guided by culture if available. Tetanus prophylaxis status should be reviewed for all bite or scratch wounds. Patients should avoid contact with immunocompromised individuals while lesions are active, though person-to-person transmission is not documented for sealpox. Cidofovir, a broad-spectrum antiviral with activity against poxviruses, has been used in rare cases of severe or persistent sealpox in immunocompromised patients, but evidence is limited to case reports and it is not a routine recommendation for healthy hosts. Surgical excision of the nodule is not recommended because it delays healing, is unnecessary given the self-limited course, and risks expanding the wound. Topical steroids should be avoided as they may suppress local immunity and prolong infection. Occupational health follow-up is recommended after return to work to assess healing, confirm lesion resolution, and ensure appropriate infection control measures are in place for both the handler and the workplace animal population.

medicalTreatments.name: Wound care and protective dressing
medicalTreatments.description: The primary treatment for uncomplicated sealpox. The lesion is cleaned with an antiseptic solution, covered with a non-adherent dressing, and kept clean and dry. Dressings are changed daily or as needed. This approach prevents secondary bacterial contamination and protects the healing wound from mechanical disruption.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for uncomplicated lesions; nearly 100% of immunocompetent patients recover fully within 4–8 weeks with this approach.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral antibiotics for secondary bacterial superinfection
medicalTreatments.description: Flucloxacillin, cefalexin, or co-amoxiclav is prescribed when secondary bacterial infection with Staphylococcus or Streptococcus species is confirmed or strongly suspected based on spreading cellulitis, purulent discharge, or systemic fever. Culture-directed therapy is preferred when available.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Antibiotic treatment resolves secondary bacterial superinfection in approximately 90–95% of cases within 5–10 days.

medicalTreatments.name: Cidofovir (for immunocompromised patients only)
medicalTreatments.description: An intravenous or topical antiviral with broad activity against double-stranded DNA poxviruses, including parapoxviruses. Reserved for immunocompromised patients with severe, persistent, or expanding sealpox lesions that do not resolve spontaneously. Not used for routine uncomplicated infections.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Limited case report evidence; approximately 70–80% response reported in immunocompromised patients with persistent poxvirus lesions in small series.

medicalTreatments.name: Tetanus prophylaxis
medicalTreatments.description: Tetanus immunization status is reviewed for all patients with seal bite or scratch wounds; booster doses or tetanus immunoglobulin are given per standard guidelines if immunization is not current.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Standard post-exposure tetanus prophylaxis is highly effective (>95%) at preventing tetanus following animal bites and puncture wounds.

alternativeTreatments.name: Imiquimod cream (off-label, immunomodulation)
alternativeTreatments.description: Imiquimod 5% cream has been used off-label in a small number of parapoxvirus nodule cases to stimulate local innate immune responses and accelerate resolution; evidence is limited to case reports and it is not a standard recommendation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Occupational health assessment and workplace modification
alternativeTreatments.description: Review of animal handling protocols, mandatory PPE policy implementation, and identification of the index animal for veterinary assessment are important non-pharmacological measures to prevent additional cases in the workplace.

whySeeSpecialist: Most cases of sealpox can be managed by a general practitioner or emergency physician with an understanding of parapoxvirus infections. Referral to an infectious disease specialist is appropriate when the patient is immunocompromised, when lesions are atypical, multiple, or fail to resolve within 8 weeks, when secondary bacterial infection is severe or does not respond to empirical antibiotics, or when laboratory confirmation of the viral diagnosis is needed for occupational health or legal purposes. A dermatologist may be consulted for skin biopsy or when the diagnosis is uncertain. Occupational medicine input is valuable to ensure appropriate workplace exposure assessment.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a general practitioner or infectious disease physician with familiarity with zoonotic and poxvirus infections, ideally with access to a virology reference laboratory for PCR confirmation. If the diagnosis is uncertain, a dermatologist experienced in unusual nodular lesions and zoonotic skin infections is appropriate. Occupational medicine physicians can assist with workplace exposure investigation and prevention programs at marine mammal facilities. For immunocompromised patients, an infectious disease specialist familiar with antiviral options for poxvirus infections should be involved.
preventionStrategies: Wear cut-resistant gloves when handling seals or sea lions, particularly when animals have visible skin lesions; this is the most effective individual preventive measure.
Inspect all animals for active skin lesions before handling; isolate animals with active sealpox and minimize direct skin contact until lesions have resolved.
Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water immediately after any pinniped contact, and before touching the face, mouth, or any broken skin.
Cover any cuts, abrasions, or broken skin on the hands with waterproof dressings before animal contact sessions.
Maintain an up-to-date tetanus immunization record; while tetanus is not caused by SPPV, animal bites carry this additional risk.
Establish occupational health reporting protocols so that all potential sealpox exposures are documented and reviewed by occupational medicine or infectious disease specialists.
Educate handlers to recognize early signs of sealpox (a new nodular lesion on the hand or forearm after animal contact) so that prompt diagnosis and work modification can be implemented.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid touching the lesion with unprotected hands, and do not attempt to squeeze, puncture, or excise the nodule at home.
Keep the lesion covered with a clean dressing at all times, especially during animal handling, to reduce contamination of the work environment.
Avoid swimming in pools or water environments that could macerate the wound and facilitate secondary bacterial infection.
Inform close contacts and household members of the diagnosis, although person-to-person transmission is not documented; standard hand hygiene is sufficient.
Report the infection to the workplace occupational health service so that the source animal can be assessed and other handlers monitored.
Maintain good general skin care — moisturizing dry, cracked skin on the hands reduces the risk of skin barrier breaches that facilitate viral entry during future animal contact.

recommended: A balanced diet with adequate protein and micronutrients to support normal wound healing and immune function during the resolution phase
Adequate vitamin C intake through fruits and vegetables, which supports collagen synthesis and skin repair
Good hydration to support normal skin healing
avoid: No specific dietary restrictions are required for sealpox; a generally healthy diet supports recovery

exerciseGuidelines: Normal physical activity can be maintained during sealpox infection, provided the lesion is adequately covered and protected from trauma. Activities that involve water immersion (swimming, water sports) should be avoided while the lesion is in the vesicular or open phase to prevent maceration and secondary infection. Return to normal animal handling duties after lesion resolution should be subject to occupational health clearance.
dailyManagement: Change the wound dressing daily using clean technique; inspect the lesion for signs of secondary bacterial infection (increasing redness, warmth, swelling, pus).
Seek medical attention promptly if spreading cellulitis, fever, or purulent discharge develops.
Keep a brief log of lesion evolution with photos to facilitate monitoring and any occupational health reporting.
Avoid contact with immunocompromised family members or patients while lesions are active, as a precautionary measure.
Inform the occupational health team at the marine mammal facility so the index animal can be examined and other handlers monitored.
Do not self-medicate with topical steroids, as corticosteroids may suppress local immunity and potentially prolong the infection.
prognosis: The prognosis of sealpox in immunocompetent individuals is excellent. All documented human cases have resolved completely without specific antiviral therapy, typically within 4–8 weeks. The lesion leaves a small, usually inconspicuous scar at the inoculation site. There is no systemic dissemination, no recurrence (as solid immunity develops after infection), and no long-term sequelae in healthy hosts. The primary risks are secondary bacterial superinfection, which responds well to antibiotics, and transmission to other handlers or animals in the workplace, which can be prevented with appropriate infection control. In immunocompromised patients, lesions may be larger and more persistent, requiring antiviral therapy, but fatal outcomes have not been reported in the literature.
recoveryTimeline: The typical clinical course progresses through five stages over 4–8 weeks: macular/papular stage (days 1–7), targetoid stage (days 7–14), acute nodule stage (weeks 2–4), regenerative stage (weeks 4–6), and dry crust/scar stage (weeks 6–8). Lesions that have not resolved or significantly regressed by 8 weeks should be reassessed for secondary infection or immunocompromise. Most patients can return to normal duties within 6–8 weeks of lesion onset, provided the area has re-epithelialized.
complications: Secondary bacterial superinfection — the most common complication; Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes superimpose on the disrupted skin, causing cellulitis or abscess that requires antibiotic treatment.

Persistent or enlarging lesions in immunocompromised individuals — while rare, impaired cellular immunity allows the virus to replicate more freely, producing lesions that fail to follow the normal self-limited course.

Erythema multiforme — a rare immune-mediated skin reaction occasionally triggered by parapoxvirus infection, producing target-shaped lesions distant from the primary site; it is self-limited but can be concerning in appearance.

Psychological impact and work disruption — the appearance of the lesion (which can resemble a serious infection or malignancy) causes significant anxiety until diagnosis is confirmed; time off work for affected handlers may have occupational and financial consequences.

Workplace transmission cluster — if the source animal is not identified and isolated, multiple handlers may be infected in sequence; outbreak management requires occupational health and veterinary coordination.

supportResources.name: Public Health England — Zoonoses: Guidance for Employers in Animal-Related Occupations

supportResources.name: USDA APHIS — Marine Mammal Health and Stranding Response

sources.title: Nettleton PF et al. Isolation and characterization of parapoxviruses from red deer (Cervus elaphus) and seals. Veterinary Record 1995; 137(8):197-199

confusedWithConditions.name: Orf (ecthyma contagiosum)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Orf is caused by the related Orf virus and produces clinically identical lesions, but is acquired from sheep, goats, or deer rather than seals; differentiation requires PCR or electron microscopy with exposure history.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pseudocowpox (milker's nodule)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pseudocowpox is acquired from dairy cattle and bovine teats; it produces the same nodular parapoxvirus lesion morphology but in an agricultural occupational setting rather than a marine mammal one.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cowpox
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cowpox is an orthopoxvirus causing more inflammatory and sometimes hemorrhagic pustular lesions, acquired mainly from cats, rodents, or cattle in Eurasia; it may cause multiple lesions and systemic illness, unlike self-limited sealpox nodules.

confusedWithConditions.name: Monkeypox
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Monkeypox (mpox) causes widespread generalized vesiculopustular rash, lymphadenopathy, and systemic illness affecting the whole body; sealpox produces only a single or few localized nodules at the animal contact site.

relatedConditions.name: Orf (ecthyma contagiosum)
relatedConditions.relevance: Caused by the most closely related parapoxvirus (Orf virus), producing clinically identical lesions in sheep and goat handlers; the prototypical parapoxvirus zoonosis against which sealpox is compared.

relatedConditions.name: Pseudocowpox (milker's nodule)
relatedConditions.relevance: Another occupational parapoxvirus zoonosis acquired from cattle; shares identical clinical presentation, pathology, and self-limited course with sealpox.

relatedConditions.name: Cowpox
relatedConditions.relevance: An orthopoxvirus zoonosis causing more severe nodular and vesiculopustular skin lesions; comparison helps clinicians understand the spectrum of poxvirus zoonotic infections.

relatedConditions.name: Monkeypox
relatedConditions.relevance: A more serious orthopoxvirus infection that causes systemic illness; important to distinguish from sealpox in patients presenting with poxvirus-type skin lesions after animal contact.

faqs.question: What is sealpox?
faqs.answer: Sealpox is a rare occupational skin infection caused by the sealpox virus, a parapoxvirus. It produces a single firm nodular lesion on the hand or forearm of people who handle infected seals or sea lions, resolving on its own within 4–8 weeks.

faqs.question: How do you get sealpox?
faqs.answer: You get sealpox through direct skin contact with an infected seal or sea lion, usually through a bite, scratch, or abrasion that allows the virus to enter the skin. It affects marine mammal handlers, veterinarians, and wildlife rehabilitators almost exclusively.

faqs.question: What does a sealpox lesion look like?
faqs.answer: Sealpox begins as a small red papule on the hand or forearm, then progresses to a vesicle (blister), then a firm reddish-blue nodule 1–3 cm across over 2–4 weeks. The nodule eventually crusts over and heals, usually leaving a small scar.

faqs.question: Is sealpox dangerous?
faqs.answer: Sealpox is not dangerous in healthy individuals. It is a self-limited infection that heals completely within 4–8 weeks without specific treatment. The main risk is secondary bacterial infection of the skin lesion, which responds to antibiotics.

faqs.question: Can sealpox spread from person to person?
faqs.answer: No. There is no documented person-to-person transmission of sealpox. The infection is acquired solely from direct contact with infected seals or sea lions and does not spread between humans.

faqs.question: How is sealpox treated?
faqs.answer: Sealpox requires no specific antiviral treatment in healthy people. Daily wound care with a clean dressing is the main management. Antibiotics are added only if secondary bacterial infection develops. The lesion resolves on its own within 4–8 weeks.

faqs.question: How is sealpox diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is usually clinical — based on the characteristic lesion on the hand after pinniped contact. Confirmation uses electron microscopy or PCR of a lesion swab, which identifies the parapoxvirus and distinguishes sealpox from orf or other poxviruses.

faqs.question: How long does a sealpox lesion last?
faqs.answer: A sealpox lesion typically takes 4–8 weeks to pass through its stages and heal completely. It progresses from a papule to a vesicle, then a firm nodule, then a crust, and finally a small scar. The process cannot be shortened significantly by any available treatment.

faqs.question: Should I see a doctor for a suspected sealpox lesion?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any new nodular skin lesion appearing after contact with a seal or sea lion should be assessed by a doctor to confirm the diagnosis, exclude secondary bacterial infection, and arrange occupational health follow-up. Treatment may be needed if infection occurs.

faqs.question: Can children get sealpox?
faqs.answer: Children can theoretically be exposed if they handle infected seals, but nearly all documented cases involve working adults with occupational pinniped contact. Members of the public who encounter stranded seals should not handle them directly for multiple reasons including zoonotic infection risk.

faqs.question: How do I prevent sealpox?
faqs.answer: Wear cut-resistant gloves when handling seals or sea lions, especially animals with visible skin lesions. Wash hands immediately after animal contact. Cover any cuts or abrasions with waterproof dressings before handling. Avoid contact with lesion-bearing animals when possible.

faqs.question: Is sealpox the same as orf?
faqs.answer: Sealpox and orf are caused by closely related parapoxviruses and produce clinically identical lesions. The key difference is the source animal: orf comes from sheep and goats, while sealpox comes from seals and sea lions. PCR or electron microscopy can distinguish them.

faqs.question: Does sealpox leave a permanent scar?
faqs.answer: A small, usually inconspicuous scar at the site of the resolved nodule is common. In most cases the scar fades significantly over months and causes no functional impairment. The scar is generally much smaller and less visible than the lesion appeared at its peak.

faqs.question: Is sealpox related to smallpox or monkeypox?
faqs.answer: Sealpox is caused by a parapoxvirus, which is a different genus from smallpox (variola) and monkeypox viruses, which are orthopoxviruses. Sealpox causes only a localized skin nodule and does not cause the widespread rash, systemic illness, or high mortality associated with smallpox or the broader skin involvement seen with monkeypox.

faqs.question: What should I tell my employer if I get sealpox?
faqs.answer: Report the diagnosis to your occupational health service immediately. The source animal needs to be assessed by a veterinarian, other handlers should be monitored, and workplace PPE protocols should be reviewed. Sealpox may be a reportable occupational disease in some jurisdictions.

faqs.question: Can sealpox be prevented with a vaccine?
faqs.answer: There is no specific vaccine for sealpox virus in humans. Smallpox vaccination (vaccinia) may provide partial cross-protection against poxviruses but is not recommended for routine occupational prevention of sealpox. PPE and hygiene protocols are the primary preventive tools.

faqs.question: Is sealpox common?
faqs.answer: Sealpox is very rare in humans. Fewer than 100 cases are documented in the published medical literature. It occurs almost exclusively in people who work closely with pinnipeds professionally, making it an occupational disease rather than a public health concern.

faqs.question: Can I go to work with a sealpox lesion?
faqs.answer: Discuss return-to-work plans with your occupational health physician. The lesion should be fully covered with a secure dressing before any animal contact. Some facilities require handlers to remain off direct animal work until the lesion has crusted over to prevent contaminating the animal environment.

faqs.question: What animals carry sealpox?
faqs.answer: Seals, sea lions, and walruses (collectively pinnipeds) are the natural animal reservoir of sealpox virus. Both wild and captive-bred pinnipeds can carry and transmit the virus. Rodents and other marine animals are not known to be significant reservoirs.

faqs.question: Does sealpox affect the immune system long-term?
faqs.answer: Sealpox produces solid immunity after infection — reinfection with the same virus is not reported. There are no known long-term effects on immune function in healthy individuals after recovery. The infection is localized to the skin and does not cause systemic immune suppression.

metaDescription: Sealpox is a rare parapoxvirus zoonosis in seal handlers causing a self-healing nodule on the hand. Learn symptoms, diagnosis and wound care management.
